items.title: Define one primary validation metric per project
items.avoid: Logging ten validation metrics and picking whichever looks best after the fact.
items.prefer: Pre-register val/f1 (classification) or val/rmse (regression) as the selection metric before the sweep starts.
items.why: Post-hoc metric shopping on validation curves still biases selection— one primary metric keeps comparisons honest.
items.example: Experiment charter: primary=val/f1, secondary=val/loss for diagnostics only— leaderboard sorts by val/f1.

items.title: Always log train and validation on paired curves
items.prefer: Log both train/loss and val/loss on the same step or epoch axis every evaluation pass.
items.why: Paired curves reveal overfitting (divergence) and underfitting (both flat)— validation alone hides whether the model is learning on train.
items.example: Dashboard panel: two series train/loss and val/loss vs epoch for run exp-042.

items.title: Keep test metrics out of the training loop
items.avoid: Adding test/f1 to the same callback that runs every epoch for val/f1.
items.prefer: Run a separate final_eval script that logs test/* once with metadata phase=final_report.
items.why: Separating test logging enforces the locked-test discipline and prevents accidental test-driven checkpoint picks.
items.example: train.py logs train/* and val/*; release.py loads best checkpoint and logs test/f1 once to the tracker.

items.title: Use shared logging helpers for naming
items.avoid: Each script inventing its own metric strings ad hoc.
items.prefer: A small log_metrics(split, name, value, step) helper that enforces train/, val/, test/ prefixes.
items.why: Centralized naming prevents drift across teammates and makes tracker dashboards stable over months of runs.
items.example: log_metrics("val", "f1", 0.72, step=epoch) always writes val/f1 regardless of framework.
